nativeRange.habitat: Submerged or semi-submerged in slow-moving or still freshwater; forms dense mats on bedrock, cobbles, root masses and submerged wood. Most commonly reported in the eastern United States (Midwest, South, Northeast) with sparse populations in western states and across temperate and Mediterranean regions of Europe, Mexico, Chile, Australia, Zambia and the Arabian Peninsula.

decorPreferences: driftwood (especially branchy or upright pieces that showcase the fan-shaped fronds)
porous rock, lava rock, seiryu, or other textured stones
stainless or plastic mesh sheets for moss walls and carpets
tied with cotton thread / fishing line or attached with cyanoacrylate gel until rhizoids anchor
never buried in substrate — the base will rot if covered
gentle to moderate flow keeps the iconic fan-shaped fronds free of detritus

goodWith: tetras, rasboras and other small community fish
biotope tanks for North American or Mediterranean streams

summary: Phoenix Moss is a visually distinctive aquarium moss (Fissidens fontanus) recognised by its compact, fan-shaped fronds of overlapping pocket-like leaves. It should be attached to driftwood, rock, or mesh rather than planted in substrate, and tolerates a wide range of light and water conditions.
careNotes:
Fissidens fontanus is one of the most visually distinctive aquarium mosses, forming compact, fan-shaped fronds of overlapping 'pocket' leaves — each leaf 2-7 mm long with a conspicuous midrib — rather than the loose, stringy growth typical of Java Moss or Christmas Moss. It is a true aquatic bryophyte (a non-vascular plant that absorbs water and nutrients directly through its fronds) and is unusual among hobby mosses for being native to North America, particularly the eastern United States, with additional populations across Europe, the Mediterranean, Mexico, Chile, Australia, and Zambia.

ATTACHMENT: Fissidens fontanus is an epiphyte and must be tied or glued to driftwood, porous rock, lava rock, or plastic/stainless mesh. Do NOT bury it in substrate — covering the base will cause it to rot. Over several weeks, the moss sends out tiny root-like anchors called rhizoids that grip the surface on their own, after which any cotton thread or fishing line can be removed.

LIGHTING: The moss tolerates low to medium light levels. It survives in low-light setups, but moderate to higher light (PAR roughly 20-60 micromoles at the moss surface) tends to produce denser, more compact fan-shaped growth.

CO2: Supplemental carbon dioxide is optional. The plant grows without it, though adding CO2 (around 20-30 ppm) noticeably increases growth rate and frond density.

SIZE AND PARAMETERS: Individual fronds typically reach 2-5 cm in height. Recommended temperature is 20-26 °C (68-79 °F), though some sources report the viable range extending to 18-28 °C; stability tends to matter more than hitting an exact value. pH 6.0-7.5; soft to moderately hard water is suitable.

SHRIMP AND FRY REFUGE: Dense Fissidens mats are excellent shelter for newly hatched fry and for dwarf-shrimp larvae, which graze the biofilm and microscopic organisms that accumulate on the fronds.

PARAMETER STABILITY: Sudden swings in water chemistry can cause patches of the moss to melt or rot, so gradual changes are advisable.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only under aquarium conditions: cut or pull off any healthy fragment of the fan and re-attach it to a new surface — every piece regrows. There is no need to wait for a reproductive cycle. True sexual reproduction (spore production from a sporophyte) requires emersed growth and is essentially never observed in fully submerged aquarium tanks.

additional.notes: Cross-verification of high-stakes fields. Used for: lighting (low), CO2 (not required, beneficial), temperature 18-25 °C / 65-77 °F (narrower than the aggregate range), pH 5.0-7.5 (wider on the acid side), slow growth rate, placement (closer to the center of the tank to showcase fountain shape; can be used as backdrop or carpet), propagation by cutting one plant into fragments that re-attach to wood/stone/plastic mesh within days, native range (North America), care difficulty (not a difficult plant to keep). Confirmed the slow growth rate and the do-not-fertilize-aggressively caveat.
